summary: The Gemara expounds each phrase of Numbers 19 regarding the red heifer, establishes that the Tsuk cliff is 12 mil from Jerusalem, details how the goat is pushed backward, and describes the signal relay system and the crimson thread that turned white as a sign of atonement.

display.whats: What does each phrase in the parah adumah verse teach, and what are the physical details of the Azazel goat ceremony?
display.hint: The Gemara is doing two things simultaneously: a verse-by-verse drasha of Numbers 19 (each word teaches a halakha about the parah rite) and filling in practical details of the Yom Kippur goat journey - the cliff distance, the manner of pushing, the signal system.

learning.summary: A verse-by-verse reading of Numbers 19 generates a long chain of halakhot about the parah, while simultaneously the Mishna's description of the Azazel journey is unpacked - the 12-mil Tsuk, the backward push, the crimson thread.
learning.ahaMoment: The crimson thread turning white is Is. 1:18 fulfilled physically - 'though your sins be red as scarlet, they shall become white as snow.' The Temple era was when this verse was literally enacted.
learning.memoryAnchor: Crimson thread + backward push + 12-mil cliff: the ceremony was designed so there would be no ambiguity that the goat had truly been sent away and could not return.
learning.openQuestion: If the thread sometimes did not turn white even in Temple times, does that mean atonement was not achieved?

narrative: Shimon HaTzaddik's thread always turned white

misconceptions: The thread not turning white does not mean atonement was denied - the sending itself achieves atonement.

crimson thread half on cliff half on goat

quizSeeds.seed: How far was the Tsuk from Jerusalem?
quizSeeds.answer: 12 mil, corresponding to the measure of the Israelite camp.

quizSeeds.seed: How was the crimson thread divided on Yom Kippur?
quizSeeds.answer: Half was tied to the cliff rock and half to the goat's horns; when the goat fell, the thread on the cliff turned white.

sugyot.finalRuling: The Tsuk is 12 mil from Jerusalem; the goat is pushed backward; the crimson thread's whitening is a sign of atonement but not its cause.

glossary.en: The cliff 12 mil from Jerusalem from which the Azazel goat was pushed on Yom Kippur.
Crimson/scarlet thread tied to the Azazel goat that would turn white as a sign of atonement, based on Is. 1:18.

rashiTranslations.en: A donkey was taken with the goat according to the first Tanna - implying the goat could ride.
The verse 'and he shall slaughter it' means he shall not slaughter another animal at the same time.
Verse: 'And Elazar the priest shall take some of its blood with his finger' - for its own sake.
The cedar wood and the hyssop and the crimson thread - all three must be present together.
The priest washes his garments and immerses his body in water.
He remains impure until evening - this is the rule for all who handle the parah ritual.
The stranger who burns the heifer: even a non-priest may burn it according to some; others disagree.
The gathering of the ashes: 'and a pure man shall gather' - must be a ritually pure person.
The ashes are deposited 'outside the camp in a pure place' - this requires a special location.
The verse specifies 'pure place' to teach that the ashes must be kept away from impurity.
Distance to the Tsuk (cliff): 12 mil from Jerusalem, matching the Israelite camp in the wilderness.
The 12 mil corresponds to the measure of the Israelite camp, as the verse says 'to the wilderness.'
The goat is pushed off the cliff backward - this is the manner of its sending to Azazel.
The goat tumbles and breaks apart before it reaches halfway down - a halakhic detail about when death occurs.
Even if the goat does not die from the fall, it is considered sent and the rite is complete.
The ish iti's impurity begins at the moment he pushes the goat - the act of sending causes it.
Why backward? So the goat cannot scramble back up and return.
Practical: the cliff is so steep that even a small push results in certain death.
The wilderness requirement: the cliff must be a desolate, uninhabited area.
The verse 'to the wilderness' implies to a place of isolation, not to a city or town.
The 10 booths spaced along the route: the first is at the Jerusalem city limit.
At each booth, the ish iti could rest and be offered food and water.
The ish iti would decline - fasting was customary for him on Yom Kippur out of honor.
At the final booth: the ish iti prepares to cross to the cliff area alone.
The cliff area itself is outside the normal limits; a non-Jew was stationed at the Tsuk.
Why a non-Jew at the Tsuk? To wave a signal cloth when the goat had gone over.
The signal was relayed back station by station to Jerusalem - 'your goat is gone.'
This signal was how people in Jerusalem knew the rite was complete before the crimson thread.
The crimson thread: half tied to the cliff rock, half to the goat's horns.
When the goat fell, its half of the thread turned white - a supernatural sign of atonement.
If it turned white: they rejoiced. If not: they were troubled, but the rite was still valid.
In Shimon HaTzaddik's time, it always turned white; later it became unreliable.
The verse 'though your sins be red as scarlet, they shall become white as snow' (Is. 1:18) is the basis.
The Gemara notes: this verse was fulfilled literally in the Temple era through the crimson thread.
After Shimon HaTzaddik's death: sometimes white, sometimes not - this caused anxiety.
Rabban Yochanan ben Zakkai, 40 years before the Destruction, observed the thread stopped turning.
He said: 'Do not rely on the thread - it is a sign but not the atonement itself.'
The actual atonement is through the goat being sent; the thread is a physical indicator.
The Gemara will now proceed to the bull's blood sprinkling in the Kodesh Kodashim.
Review: 43a covered the parah verse exposition, the Tsuk distance, and the goat's manner of death.
The page also established the signal relay system from Tsuk back to Jerusalem.
Key halacha: the cliff must be 12 mil away; pushing backward; impurity begins at act of pushing.
The crimson thread's whitening is a sign of atonement, not the atonement itself.
The booth system shows the communal infrastructure built around this annual ceremony.
The ish iti is treated with honor and provided for throughout his journey.
His fasting is a custom of piety, not a requirement - he may eat if needed.
The Tsuk cliff: the Gemara identifies it with modern topography based on the 12-mil measurement.
Other opinions about which cliff is intended; the main point is the distance requirement.
The goat pushed backward: various explanations for this unusual manner.
One explanation: to prevent the goat from turning back and clinging to the cliff edge.
Another: the backward push symbolizes rejection and sending away - 'l'Azazel' means sent outward.
The verse says 'and he shall send it away to the wilderness by the hand of a man in his time.'
The 'man in his time' is the ish iti - designated for this task specifically.
No one else may perform this act; if the ish iti dies, a replacement must be found immediately.
The Gemara examines what happens if the ish iti becomes incapacitated mid-journey.
Answer: another person takes over - the sending is what matters, not who specifically does it.
But the impurity falls on whoever actually performs the sending act.
Final ruling on 43a: all details of the Tsuk journey are confirmed; ish iti impurity is deliberate.
Transition: with the goat sent, the High Priest returns to the Temple for the incense service.
The incense offering inside the Holy of Holies is the next and most private act of Yom Kippur.
No one may be in the Temple when the incense is offered - absolute solitude required.
This will be the focus of 43b-44b: the incense laws and who must leave during the offering.
The Gemara prepares by completing all the goat-ceremony halakhot first.
43a is a dense legal exposition of Numbers 19 verses combined with Yom Kippur avodah details.
The parah adumah and the Azazel goat share important structural parallels explored throughout these daf.
